sentences.text: Yes.
Well, the usual procedure.
They cut our hair, all hair-- on your head, pubic hair, under your arms.
They took away everything you had.
I mean, I remember clutching some photographs in my left hand.
I don't where I got them, from my purse probably.
And everything taken away.
And we had a shower.
No gas, shower, real shower.
And afterwards, we got lager clothes.
I never had a zebra.
I had a gray, shirt-like dress, like a blouse, but it went to my knees.
And some girls got the same gray dress, no, no belt, just a button here.
That was, I don't know, five sizes too big.
Others got one that they could barely get into, so it was big changing around.
And I had such gorgeous shoes, handmade, the luxury of my life at that time, handmade shoes.
And of course those were gone.
And I didn't get clogs.
Most people got clogs, you know those wooden shoes, very hard to walk in.
I admire these kids who, these days, it's very fashionable.
But evidently they are made differently.
I never tried them.
I wouldn't wear them if you gave them to me.
But I got some miserable black high shoes, you know, to my ankle.
And they were slightly small.
No stockings, no underwear, no nothing.
And we looked at each other, we didn't recognize each other.
It was horrible.
And then they took us to our block, which is the barracks, called them blocks.
And later on, I found out that these blocks were for about 450 to 500 people.
We were 1,450, give or take 5%.
I never counted, [LAUGHS] really.
Three to a [NON-ENGLISH]-- three to a cot or to a space.
So called mattresses-- so called.
Straw mattresses, you know, that material that potato sacks used to be made of, before nylon was invented, OK?
Very rough kind of material.
And it was supposed to have straw.
I don't know how many inches, well, there was no straw.
I don't know whether the lice ate the straw or whatever, but there was no straw.
So it was hard, it was very hard.
And literally, if you wanted to turn, everybody had to turn.
Because otherwise, there was no space.
Many times we slept this way, meaning my head was between two pairs of feet, and my feet were between two, two heads on the other side.
Three, three levels.
Somebody told me that they started with two levels, then business got too good, so they built third level.

And I was on the third level through sheer luck, which was an advantage, because if you were on the lower levels, then many times when people would go to the bathroom, it would all fall down on the second level, or from second level to what.
It was really unbelievable.
How did we-- and we lived.
We survived.
Very few suicides in all concentration camps, very few.
Interesting.
Life is precious, if only people would be aware of that.
Oh, I tell you.
[SIGHS] There was one gal by the name of Lily.
I have no idea about her last name, never knew.
And she was feeble-minded.
And she had the best time of us all.
Because when we stood appel-- five in a row and don't move, OK-- she would step out and start to go dancing around and singing around.
And her nose was running.
And she was menstruating, which none of us were.
Thin girl, nuts.
She couldn't-- she didn't know where she was.
She felt like dancing, she was dancing around.
And the Germans favored her, because Lily was entertainment.
And last year I got hold of a book, I don't remember the name or title.
The first time somebody wrote a book about how these strange, slightly lucky people survived.
The clowns of the camps.
But finally, eventually, she went up the chimney.
But you know, she survived for so long.
And then the Germans would give her the best of the soup.
Or sometimes even bring her, I don't know, a piece of bread.
Come on Lily.
You know, like you say, hey Fifi, and you whistle to your dog.
They say, hey Lily, come on, dance.
And she did.
And she smiled, had ugly teeth.
She's smiling.
So this is one inhabitant, one colleague.
Across for me was Jadja, a Polish girl, Polish resistance.
And once in a blue moon, when nobody else could hear us, we would exchange-- you know, I don't know how we found out about each other, by smelling, or how-- that what Jadja did, and what I did.
And we had some similarities in, in our activities and so on.
That was Jadja.
Then there was one girl who had a beautiful voice.
I mean a God-given, beautiful, absolutely beautiful voice.
And she would sing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And her favorite song was 'Jealousy.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I learned at that time 'Jealousy,' I think, in about three languages.
Because other people knew the same song.
Not English.
And so I remember her singing.
Then there was a whole cooking group, very important.
And I mean, seriously, very important.
It sort of kept up morale.
Because we tried to tell about a book we remember, just sort of entertain each other, to, to, to talk about sane things in-- in life.
Because we were living through insanity.
And this cooking group would cook and would say, well, take two eggs.
No, the others said, you must take three.
There was big fight.
Two eggs or three eggs, you know.
No eggs in sight of course.
And they would make the dumplings, finally.
In about 10 minutes they made the dumpling.
Then they would serve the dumplings and describe the plate and the pattern of the plate and this fork is a little bit bent, and-- But the dumplings, the Czech national dish, the dumpling.
Fruit dumplings.
And the butter, and how the rivulets of the butter flowed down the side.
And whoops, here, that-- that one drop of butter, got stuck.
But the next one came and pushed it down.
And the rest of us were sitting there, you know, our mouths watering because we were constantly hungry, of course.
But this cooking business, it's not special to, to my experience.
That went on everywhere, even among the men.
They were also quote unquote, 'cooking,' everyone, everybody.
It-- looking back, now that I've survived, you know, everything is very interesting.
The story I wanted to tell, and I'll try to make it very short, about what life is all about.
After the war, I went back to this little town-- not little, just town-- where I used to go to a-- the milk bar would be the closest in, in English concept.
They were selling yogurt or milk and some sweets, you know.
And I had my sweet, and then I had my yogurt.
And I go to pay, and there is the same gal who was there before the war, Maria, or whatever her name was.
And I said very casually, paying my bill, well Mary, where were you during the war?
And she said to me, right here, dearie, right here.
And I said, thank God.
I wouldn't switch with her, with Auschwitz, with everything else, I wouldn't switch with her.
Five years or more, she sat on that orange crate, or what, so her feet wouldn't get cold, saying, 2.50, thank you very much, come again. 3.50, thank you very much, come again.
No, no.
So it all depends.
Somebody would be very unhappy with my life and happy with Mary's life, saying, 2.50 thank you very much, come again, in the milk bar.
So, the meaning of life, or the purpose of life.
What did they give you to eat in Auschwitz?

Well, at one point, at the lowest point in Auschwitz-- not in the other camp-- somebody said, somebody in the know said, 890 calories a day, which is very hard to believe until you will hear what it was.
In the morning a liquid, brownish in color, that came sometimes under the title of coffee, sometimes under the title of tea, but it was always the same damn thing.
And very seldom was it warm.
It was supposed to be warm, but by the time they brought it, I don't know, half a mile from the kitchen-- in huge cauldrons, two and two women had to bring it-- that's it.
Then came lunch.
We got a piece of bread.
I don't know how many ounces, but it was like a slice of-- the size only, size of a slice of American bread.
I don't know how many ounces that will be.
But not soft white bread, but heavy.
And first you looked at it from both sides and see what-- who is there, you know?
Because sometimes there was pieces of cockroaches, of-- everything went into it.
And some claim that it was sawdust mixed with the flour.
I don't know, I ate it.
I ate everything that was edible.
And with that we got sometimes a pat of butter-- or margarine, I imagine, margarine-- or a pat of marmalade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or maybe once or twice we got a slice of a salami with the-- radius-- with the-- what is it called, not the radius.
What is two radiuses?
Anyhow, how wide a circle is, maybe inch and a half, thin.
Just barely-- you got a taste, that was it.
And in the evening it was more-- was soup.
Sometimes for lunch it was soup, sometimes for evening it was soup.
And the soup was warmer than the coffee.

And if we were lucky, we got something from the bottom, because all kinds of things were swimming in the soup-- potatoes, potato peels, which was very funny because lately we found out, no, that the potato peels should be eaten.
And [LAUGHS] few years ago, for the first time I saw in the frozen goods in American grocery supermarket potato peels.
And I started laughing.
A lot people looked, what the hell is she laughing about.
And I just couldn't stop.
That potato peels-- I say hey, Auschwitz.
They learn something.
Anyhow.
So potato peels or some vegetable, maybe a carrot, or maybe some other vegetable that they got hold of.
But you know these things sink down.
And there was the kapo who was distributing with a sort of half globe-shaped dish she was distr-- or sometimes she had a ladle.
And she would take it from the top, so you got nothing swimming.
And when Lily came around, then she would reach lower.
Or she would say, Lily, wait until I get to the bottom.
And there were the goodies-- the potato peels or vegetables or whatever.
Meat, very seldom.
Then when we were in, in the-- in Gross-Rosen and where we were on work kommando, we got better food.
Also that was already '44, '45, and the Germans were getting a hell of a beating.
And the moment that the news from the front was bad for the Germans, we were better off.
Because the German supervisor suddenly became human and brought us a piece of bread or maybe a half rotten apple or a potato.
We could tell how the war is going by the behavior of the German supervisor.
When they started kicking us and, and, and cursing us and being rough with us, we knew the Germans are winning again.
Maybe just one battle.
And when the, the tide turned, suddenly they were nice.
Insurance, you know, took out the insurance.
I brought a prisoner an apple.
I'm not a Nazi.
[DRINKS]
Appels, yeah.
Well first of all, they started at dawn.
Most of the time it was still dark.
And that was true with Auschwitz.
That was true in the tunnel, all over the place.
I think they woke up us at 5:00.
And then appel started at 6:00.
For no good reason, because we didn't start marching to the factory except maybe a few minutes before 7:00.
And work started at 7:00.
And the joke is really that the German supervisors had to get up, too, with us.
So I don't see why they didn't change that.
Well, anyhow.
And then we worked in the factory.
And we had about half an hour for so-called lunch.
And then we continued.
And you add up 7 and 12 is 19.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, 7 and 12 is 19, which means 7:00 PM.
We didn't work till 7:00 PM.
So it must have been just an 11 hour, because I remember at 6:00 it was end, and we came back to the barracks.
And there was--
--Dinner at the barracks.
No, no, unfortunately I wasn't on a work detail in Auschwitz.
Luckily, I didn't know at that time that that's dangerous.
[LAUGHS] No, the work detail was in the two other camps.
Oh the appel-- the-- we were just hanging around, very dangerous, very dangerous.
And we were-- if there-- they needed some occasional work, they would come and say, I need 50 to clean this latrine.
I need 100 to move the stones or whatever, you know.
And that was very dangerous.
Because then that they need space, these not assigned kommandos go first to the gas chamber, for no other reason that we need the space.
Weeding out, you know?
Like I weed out my library, or you weed out your garden.
No, but in, in Gross-Rosen I worked in a textile factory.
And that was-- didn't I describe that to you over the phone?
Because I recall somewhere that not long ago I described those big spools like that.
OK, I'm going to bring you back to Auschwitz for a moment.
Yeah.
Oh yes, well, yeah.
Because the next whole block from me on one side was Jewish prisoners.
And the other side were non-Jewish prisoners.
And I'll come back to the Jews in a second.
It was interesting.
That other block, they had little windows, and I talked to some women, older women, and you could tell they were country women, not city folk, from Lidice.
And so I am one of those few who talked to the women of Lidice in Auschwitz.
Because everywhere you read that the Lidice women were taken to Ravensbruck, which is true.
But evidently some were taken to Auschwitz.
I talked to them.
Anyhow.
So on the other side were, were Jewish, Jewish women, Jewish prisoners.
And there was a sort of a yard, open space in between blocks, maybe as wide as twice this room.
And so we could talk, we could talk.
And they had-- I think they had a harder time.
Because they lived with where is my husband, where is my child?
Where are my parents, you know, which was one-- one trouble we didn't have.
Were you aware that you were being treated any differently from any of the other prisoners?
No, not really.
Except over them, constantly, hang the danger of the gas chamber.
Constantly.
And with us, it was sort of mezzo mezzo.
We might, we might not.
You know, several-- several hundreds of thousands of non-Jews went up the chimney.
The first people went up the chimney were non-Jews.
The test, the test, yeah.
And the damn chimneys were there constantly, and the soot was falling on us.
On the third floor cot where I was-- where my space was, in the back, there was a little opening maybe this big, with some wiring.
But I could see out.
And I could see one chimney.
And I could see parts-- you know, like an inch here, an inch there-- of the road from the railroad station to the chimney.

And on several occasions, the time coincided that I was on my cot and looked out, and there were groups of people in dress-- you know, I mean, in clothing-- coming from the station, going to the gas chamber.

And that is the reason why I'm saying this-- and many other things that we observed, of course, in Auschwitz-- is the reason that the best testimonials about the Holocaust, about confirming yes, there was a Holocaust and none of this idiotic nonsense that there was no Holocaust.
Well, the lunatic fringe is here.
We are the best of witnesses.
Because we are the so-called objective witnesses.
We are not Jewish, but we saw it, you know.
Not mentioning the Sonderkommando, which sometimes was Jewish and sometimes was not Jewish.
And some of them survived and wrote their memoirs.
So this lunatic fringe, and I thought that by now they stop, but they don't.
I think it was block four, but I wouldn't swear to it.
Very low number.
I think the Lidice women were three, we were four, and the Jews were five.
But I'm not sure.
You know, I went back to Auschwitz, and I was absolutely lost-- Birkenau, really, not, not Auschwitz-- because the barracks don't stand anywhere.
They had to take them down, so they say.
And they have one or two of those-- we call them blocks and now they call them barracks-- left.
And I went in and it didn't look like my barrack.
There was lots of brick work.
I mean, there is lots of brick work in those sample blocks.
We didn't have any brick, I am positive I am positive.
And at that time I still had a friend who was with me there.
And I called her over the phone.
I said, you remember bricks?
She said what bricks?
No bricks.
So they left evidently some better barracks or rebuilt them, I don't know.
But all those dozens of barracks-- have you been to Auschwitz?
Oh, you have.
So you would know.
The-- the chimney, little chimney parts are sort of sticking out of the grass, OK?
I don't know, dozens upon dozens.
I couldn't find my block.
And I didn't see where was the-- the lager street, nothing.
It was very, very strange that-- you know, I said, I'll never forget that.
And I couldn't locate it.
I couldn't-- absolutely couldn't locate it, whether it was on this side, or that side.
Yeah, I had a number.
I used to remember, five digits.
I know that the end was 979.
I like those numbers.
But I don't remember the whole number.
Maybe it was 31-- I, I don't really remember.
Well, [DRINKS] you know, when you are in jail, ask any jailbird in the war or outside, they are the types of friendships that are very special.
And we maintained contact for a long time with the four of us.
[SPEAKING CZECH] Yeah, the four of us out of the five stood appel whenever possible together.
But you know, most of those are gone.
One is still alive, in Yugoslavia, of all places.
And we are in touch, we are in touch.
Her name is Vera, too.
So she was Verice, which means little Vera, and I was Vera.
Vera Kersovan.
She's in the book, chapter is about her.
Verice, somebody should write a book about her life.
More incredible [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] than a soap opera.
There's a happy ending, except he just died last year.
And I cannot talk about it, because she is alive and they are very intimate things.
But you read part of it, you read in the-- in the book, because she approved what's-- what's in the book.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Well, I might remember the names.
But-- but they are also-- you know, dead.
One, one whose name I don't remember, I don't know why, we called Little Currant, little Currant.
She was-- I don't know, she didn't know.
How old are you?
I don't know.
I don't think she knew what it means to be so old or so young.
She was a little thing like mercury, you know.
Lively eyes.
She must have been 12 or so, or maybe 11.
But she was a child.
When I was 12, I was not a child.
I could think.
She didn't know how to read and write.
A Romanian.
Romanian!
How she got to that place.
And I taught her.
I tried you know, to teach her, in the dust of the-- of the-- that little yard between the block.
Taught her numbers and taught her to read and write.
Then I drew her a picture of a clock.
Because in one place in-- not in Auschwitz, that was after Auschwitz-- in Gross-Rosen, there was a clock.
We could tell what hour it was.
Big deal, big deal!
And I taught her how to read the clock.
And I told the little idiot how to find me after the war.
She loved me.
She thought I am the cat's meow.
And she was the best organizer of all of us.
She could steal that you wouldn't believe.
She would steal the eyes out of your face and potatoes and whatever.
And I told her how to find me after the war-- two of them were-- three of us were so young, but I was the grown up youngster, and they were the kid youngsters.
And the other one, this Yugoslav girl, was an intelligent girl.
And she remembered you know, the university address in Prague.
And she wrote to me, and ever since we are in touch.
And she was here, and I was there.
And this other one, this Romanian, pshew, disappeared.
So I hope that she found some family, or I don't know.
[SIGHS]
I left Auschwitz on my birthday in 1944.
And the rea-- that was very interesting.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They always asked for volunteers-- which is sort of [LAUGHS] strange thing in Auschwitz, ask prisoners for volunteers-- for volunteers to work in a gardening outfit or in a candy factory, you know, all the goodies.
And of course we didn't believe what they are saying.
And in Auschwitz whatever you volunteered for was 50%, the other 50 being the chimney, you know, weeding out for the chimney.
So then I said, if they will ask for volunteers, for volunteers on my birthday, I will volunteer.
Because I figured few people die on their birthdays.
Anyhow.
So then came, again, volunteers, we need volunteers for textile factory.
My birthday, I volunteer.
And with me, our appel roll, and some-- a whole bunch of us volunteered.
And we won.
The 50 went out of Auschwitz rather than up the chimney.
But could have gone any, any other way.
And that's how I got marched out of Auschwitz gloriously.
And we saw color for the first time in a long time.
Because Auschwitz was not even gray.
You know, gray can be beautiful.
You know the color of mud?
It's not gray.
It's sort of a tannish gray, no?
That's the color of Auschwitz-- the barracks, our uniforms, mostly the mud or the dust, the-- or the snow when it became dirty.
[DRINKS] Where were you taken?
And we went by train-- I mean real train, we could sit.
We went to Gross-Rosen.
And Gross-Rosen is, how should I tell you?
You know where Dresden is in southeast Germany, former East Germany?
So, that neck of the woods, OK?
Now Gross-Rosen is the center.
And what I didn't know until a few years ago, when East Germany was free, then my husband and I went to all these concentration camps in former East Germany.
So I went back to Gross-Rosen-- beautiful memorials and what have you, museum.
And what I didn't know until I saw in the museum there, a map-- and there was Gross-Rosen, 63 subdivisions. 63, 63.
That's more than Auschwitz.
Auschwitz had, I think, 39.
So I was in one of those 63.
And we were taken to a textile factory.
And actually it was a spinning factory.
We had two buildings.
One was the actual textile, you know, weaving.
And the other one was the spinning.
And that's where I worked.
I'm going to stop you for a tape change.
Again!
